narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, an LSAT score of 35, middle-family income background, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school decile rankings from early years—3rd decile in Year 1 and 2nd decile in Year 3. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of past students, predicted a 40.4% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which tipped the decision to reject your application since the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed in practice. This prediction isn't a judgment on your potential or character, but a data-driven assessment based on patterns from students like you. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which placed you in the lower half compared to the typical student's 5.5-ish average—this stood out as the top signal suggesting challenges in sustaining strong academic performance heading into law school. Nearly balancing that was your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, which is actually above the average of 3.21 and strongly pulled the prediction in the favorable direction, showing the model recognized your solid college record as a key strength. Your LSAT score of 35, while slightly below the average of 36.7, was the third-biggest influence and also nudged things positively toward expecting you to pass, highlighting it as another asset despite not being at the very top. Overall, your lower Year 3 decile outweighed these positives just enough to raise concerns about bar exam readiness, especially when compared to typical admitted students who often rank higher in later school years. Your full-time status aligns with most students (over 90%), so it didn't sway things much, nor did your middle-income background. This close call—40.4% failure risk—means you're right on the edge, not far from profiles that get approved. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting metrics like later-year grades or retaking the LSAT for a higher score, perhaps through targeted prep courses tailored for Hispanic students navigating similar paths. Consider gaining practical experience, like internships at legal aid organizations serving your community, to build a standout narrative beyond numbers. Many students in your position reapply successfully after addressing these gaps—your above-average GPA shows you have the foundation to turn this around. Keep pushing forward; law needs voices like yours.
